scoreChangeLog.summary: RealYld is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: 5-Year Breakeven Inflation
assets.mainConflict: BLS CPI energy pressure (bls, +2.00) vs BLS payroll growth (bls, -1.00)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 28 counted rows. Top driver: BLS CPI energy pressure (bls, +2.00). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Primary effect: Policy rates affect 5Y BE mainly through currency, financing-cost, and demand pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 5Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 5Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Primary effect: The yield curve affects 5Y BE through growth expectations, policy tightness, and recession-risk signaling.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Real yields affect 5Y BE through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Primary effect: Breakevens are a direct market-implied inflation input for 5Y BE; rising breakevens mean stronger inflation-expectation pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Primary effect: PCE affects 5Y BE through the Fed-policy and inflation-expectations channel.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Primary effect: Growth affects 5Y BE through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Consumer demand affects 5Y BE through growth expectations and policy reaction.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Credit spreads affect 5Y BE through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Financial conditions affect 5Y BE through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for 5Y BE. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 5Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 5Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 5Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 5Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 5Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 5Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 5Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 5Y BE. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 5Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 5Y BE. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 5Y BE. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 5Y BE. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 5Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 5Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: EIA petroleum inventory balance
factors.derived: U.S. commercial crude oil stocks excluding SPR (W_EPC0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest 499,740 thousand barrels on 2016-09-30; previous 502,716 thousand barrels on 2016-09-23. Weekly change: -2,976 thousand barrels. | U.S. motor gasoline stocks (W_EPM0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None. | U.S. distillate fuel oil stocks (W_EPD0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: EIA energy tightness can affect 5-Year Breakeven Inflation through inflation pressure, rate expectations, and risk appetite. It is not the main direct driver.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger growth usually supports higher yield pressure for 5-Year Breakeven Inflation; weaker growth usually reduces rate pressure but can raise recession risk.

factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger growth usually supports higher yield pressure for 5-Year Breakeven Inflation; weaker growth usually reduces rate pressure but can raise recession risk.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis API

factors.name: BEA savings rate
factors.derived: Personal saving rate (SAVING_RATE) latest 3.00% for 2026M05; previous 3.00% for 2026M04; change 0.00 pts.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: BEA savings rate is useful macro context for 5-Year Breakeven Inflation, but it is not scored as a direct driver yet.

factors.effect: Primary effect: A higher/restrictive effective fed funds rate supports higher policy-rate pressure for 5-Year Breakeven Inflation; easing reduces that pressure.

factors.effect: Secondary effect: Easier liquidity can reduce upward yield pressure for 5-Year Breakeven Inflation; tighter liquidity can increase funding/rate pressure.

scoreChangeLog.summary: 5Y BE is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: 10-Year Breakeven Inflation
assets.mainConflict: BLS CPI energy pressure (bls, +2.00) vs BLS payroll growth (bls, -1.00)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 28 counted rows. Top driver: BLS CPI energy pressure (bls, +2.00). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Primary effect: Policy rates affect 10Y BE mainly through currency, financing-cost, and demand pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Primary effect: The yield curve affects 10Y BE through growth expectations, policy tightness, and recession-risk signaling.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Real yields affect 10Y BE through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Primary effect: Breakevens are a direct market-implied inflation input for 10Y BE; rising breakevens mean stronger inflation-expectation pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Primary effect: PCE affects 10Y BE through the Fed-policy and inflation-expectations channel.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Primary effect: Growth affects 10Y BE through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Consumer demand affects 10Y BE through growth expectations and policy reaction.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Credit spreads affect 10Y BE through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Financial conditions affect 10Y BE through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for 10Y BE. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 10Y BE. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 10Y BE. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 10Y BE. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 10Y BE. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y BE. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: EIA petroleum inventory balance
factors.derived: U.S. commercial crude oil stocks excluding SPR (W_EPC0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest 499,740 thousand barrels on 2016-09-30; previous 502,716 thousand barrels on 2016-09-23. Weekly change: -2,976 thousand barrels. | U.S. motor gasoline stocks (W_EPM0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None. | U.S. distillate fuel oil stocks (W_EPD0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: EIA energy tightness can affect 10-Year Breakeven Inflation through inflation pressure, rate expectations, and risk appetite. It is not the main direct driver.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: BLS CPI pressure
factors.derived: CPI all items (CUSR0000SA0) latest 332.57 on 2026-06-01; year-over-year change 3.73%; 3-month annualized pace 2.78%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Stronger inflation usually supports higher U.S. rate pressure for 10-Year Breakeven Inflation because markets expect tighter policy or firmer nominal yields; softer inflation reduces that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.derived: PPI final demand (WPUFD4) latest 157.04 on 2026-06-01; year-over-year change 5.51%; 3-month annualized pace 6.24%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Stronger inflation usually supports higher U.S. rate pressure for 10-Year Breakeven Inflation because markets expect tighter policy or firmer nominal yields; softer inflation reduces that pressure.

factors.name: BLS core PPI pressure
factors.derived: PPI final demand less foods, energy, and trade services (WPUFD49116) latest 142.70 on 2026-06-01; year-over-year change 5.06%; 3-month annualized pace 5.92%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Stronger inflation usually supports higher U.S. rate pressure for 10-Year Breakeven Inflation because markets expect tighter policy or firmer nominal yields; softer inflation reduces that pressure.

factors.name: BLS unemployment rate
factors.derived: Unemployment rate (LNS14000000) latest 4.20 percent on 2026-06-01; one-month change -0.10 percent; year-over-year change -2.33%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Strong labor data usually supports 10-Year Breakeven Inflation rate pressure because it can keep Fed policy firmer; weak labor usually reduces rate pressure but can raise recession concerns.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS payroll growth
factors.derived: Total nonfarm payroll employment (CES0000000001) latest 158,984.00 thousands of jobs on 2026-06-01; one-month change 57.00 thousands of jobs; year-over-year change 0.32%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Strong labor data usually supports 10-Year Breakeven Inflation rate pressure because it can keep Fed policy firmer; weak labor usually reduces rate pressure but can raise recession concerns.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS wage pressure
factors.derived: Average hourly earnings private employees (CES0500000003) latest 37.64 dollars/hour on 2026-06-01; one-month change 0.13 dollars/hour; year-over-year change 3.52%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Strong labor data usually supports 10-Year Breakeven Inflation rate pressure because it can keep Fed policy firmer; weak labor usually reduces rate pressure but can raise recession concerns.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS labor participation
factors.derived: Labor force participation rate (LNS11300000) latest 61.50 percent on 2026-06-01; one-month change -0.30 percent; year-over-year change -1.44%.

scoreChangeLog.summary: 10Y BE is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: S&P 500 Fundamental Pressure
assets.mainConflict: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +2.10) vs Census building permits (census, -1.50)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 32 counted rows. Top driver: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +2.10). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Higher policy rates usually pressure SPX500 by raising discount rates and tightening liquidity; easier policy usually helps.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on SPX500. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on SPX500. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on SPX500. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising real yields usually pressure SPX500 by increasing discount-rate pressure; falling real yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect SPX500 through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hotter PCE usually pressures SPX500 through tighter policy expectations; cooler PCE usually supports risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger growth usually supports SPX500 through earnings and demand, unless it also pushes yields too high.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger consumer demand supports SPX500 through earnings and growth; weak demand pressures risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider credit spreads usually pressure SPX500 by signaling funding stress and risk-off conditions; tighter spreads usually support risk.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Primary effect: Tighter financial conditions usually pressure SPX500; easier conditions usually support risk appetite and valuation support.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for SPX500. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on SPX500. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on SPX500. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on SPX500. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on SPX500. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on SPX500. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on SPX500. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on SPX500. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on SPX500. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on SPX500. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on SPX500. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on SPX500. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on SPX500. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on SPX500. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on SPX500. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Traders in Financial Futures - Futures Only for E-MINI S&P 500 - CHICAGO MERCANTILE EXCHANGE on 2026-07-14: Leveraged Funds long 135,454, short 500,456, net -365,002 (-18.80% of open interest), effective for this asset -18.80%.
factors.effect: Leveraged Funds are net short in the related equity-index futures contract, which is pressuring for risk appetite. Effective positioning equals -18.80% of open interest. Net long exposure supports participation; net short exposure reflects pressure or hedging.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change -3,127; effective weekly change -0.16% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for S&P 500 Fundamental Pressure. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 73.60%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

assets.name: Nasdaq 100 Fundamental Pressure
assets.mainConflict: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +2.10) vs Census building permits (census, -1.50)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 32 counted rows. Top driver: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +2.10). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Higher policy rates usually pressure NASDAQ100 by raising discount rates and tightening liquidity; easier policy usually helps.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NASDAQ100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NASDAQ100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NASDAQ100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

assets.name: Russell 2000 Fundamental Pressure
assets.mainConflict: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +2.10) vs Census building permits (census, -1.50)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 32 counted rows. Top driver: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +2.10). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Higher policy rates usually pressure RUSSELL2000 by raising discount rates and tightening liquidity; easier policy usually helps.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on RUSSELL2000. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on RUSSELL2000. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on RUSSELL2000. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising real yields usually pressure RUSSELL2000 by increasing discount-rate pressure; falling real yields usually ease that pressure.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation

factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect RUSSELL2000 through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

assets.name: Dow Jones Industrial Average Fundamental Pressure
assets.mainConflict: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +2.10) vs Census building permits (census, -1.50)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 32 counted rows. Top driver: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +2.10). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Higher policy rates usually pressure DOW by raising discount rates and tightening liquidity; easier policy usually helps.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on DOW. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on DOW. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on DOW. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising real yields usually pressure DOW by increasing discount-rate pressure; falling real yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect DOW through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hotter PCE usually pressures DOW through tighter policy expectations; cooler PCE usually supports risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger growth usually supports DOW through earnings and demand, unless it also pushes yields too high.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger consumer demand supports DOW through earnings and growth; weak demand pressures risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider credit spreads usually pressure DOW by signaling funding stress and risk-off conditions; tighter spreads usually support risk.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Primary effect: Tighter financial conditions usually pressure DOW; easier conditions usually support risk appetite and valuation support.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for DOW. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on DOW. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on DOW. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on DOW. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on DOW. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on DOW. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.effect: Low direct effect on DOW. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on DOW. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition

factors.effect: Low direct effect on DOW. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on DOW. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on DOW. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on DOW. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Traders in Financial Futures - Futures Only for DJIA Consolidated - CHICAGO BOARD OF TRADE on 2026-07-14: Leveraged Funds long 10,010, short 12,788, net -2,778 (-3.63% of open interest), effective for this asset -3.63%.
factors.effect: Leveraged Funds are net short in the related equity-index futures contract, which is pressuring for risk appetite. Effective positioning equals -3.63% of open interest. Net long exposure supports participation; net short exposure reflects pressure or hedging.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change -972; effective weekly change -1.27% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Dow Jones Industrial Average Fundamental Pressure. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 86.80%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

assets.name: Germany 40 / DAX Fundamental Pressure
assets.mainConflict: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +1.00) vs Fed Treasury General Account (treasury, -0.48)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 11 counted rows. Top driver: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +1.00). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Higher policy rates usually pressure GER40 by raising discount rates and tightening liquidity; easier policy usually helps.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising real yields usually pressure GER40 by increasing discount-rate pressure; falling real yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect GER40 through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hotter PCE usually pressures GER40 through tighter policy expectations; cooler PCE usually supports risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger growth usually supports GER40 through earnings and demand, unless it also pushes yields too high.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger consumer demand supports GER40 through earnings and growth; weak demand pressures risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider credit spreads usually pressure GER40 by signaling funding stress and risk-off conditions; tighter spreads usually support risk.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Primary effect: Tighter financial conditions usually pressure GER40; easier conditions usually support risk appetite and valuation support.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for GER40. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product supplied / demand
factors.derived: EIA product supplied for gasoline, distillates, and total products
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on GER40. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on GER40. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on GER40. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on GER40. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on GER40. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

scoreChangeLog.summary: GER40 is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: FTSE / UK100 Fundamental Pressure
assets.mainConflict: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +1.00) vs Fed Treasury General Account (treasury, -0.48)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 11 counted rows. Top driver: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +1.00). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Higher policy rates usually pressure UK100 by raising discount rates and tightening liquidity; easier policy usually helps.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising real yields usually pressure UK100 by increasing discount-rate pressure; falling real yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect UK100 through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hotter PCE usually pressures UK100 through tighter policy expectations; cooler PCE usually supports risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger growth usually supports UK100 through earnings and demand, unless it also pushes yields too high.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger consumer demand supports UK100 through earnings and growth; weak demand pressures risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider credit spreads usually pressure UK100 by signaling funding stress and risk-off conditions; tighter spreads usually support risk.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Primary effect: Tighter financial conditions usually pressure UK100; easier conditions usually support risk appetite and valuation support.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for UK100. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories

factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories

factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
