factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on UK100. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on UK100. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on UK100. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on UK100. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on UK100. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

scoreChangeLog.summary: UK100 is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: Nikkei 225 Fundamental Pressure
assets.mainConflict: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +1.00) vs Fed Treasury General Account (treasury, -0.48)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 11 counted rows. Top driver: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +1.00). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Higher policy rates usually pressure NIKKEI by raising discount rates and tightening liquidity; easier policy usually helps.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising real yields usually pressure NIKKEI by increasing discount-rate pressure; falling real yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect NIKKEI through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hotter PCE usually pressures NIKKEI through tighter policy expectations; cooler PCE usually supports risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger growth usually supports NIKKEI through earnings and demand, unless it also pushes yields too high.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger consumer demand supports NIKKEI through earnings and growth; weak demand pressures risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider credit spreads usually pressure NIKKEI by signaling funding stress and risk-off conditions; tighter spreads usually support risk.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Primary effect: Tighter financial conditions usually pressure NIKKEI; easier conditions usually support risk appetite and valuation support.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for NIKKEI. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product supplied / demand
factors.derived: EIA product supplied for gasoline, distillates, and total products
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on NIKKEI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on NIKKEI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on NIKKEI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on NIKKEI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NIKKEI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: BLS CPI pressure

scoreChangeLog.summary: NIKKEI is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: China 50 Fundamental Pressure
assets.mainConflict: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +1.00) vs Fed Treasury General Account (treasury, -0.48)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 11 counted rows. Top driver: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +1.00). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Higher policy rates usually pressure CHINA50 by raising discount rates and tightening liquidity; easier policy usually helps.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising real yields usually pressure CHINA50 by increasing discount-rate pressure; falling real yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect CHINA50 through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hotter PCE usually pressures CHINA50 through tighter policy expectations; cooler PCE usually supports risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger growth usually supports CHINA50 through earnings and demand, unless it also pushes yields too high.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger consumer demand supports CHINA50 through earnings and growth; weak demand pressures risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider credit spreads usually pressure CHINA50 by signaling funding stress and risk-off conditions; tighter spreads usually support risk.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Primary effect: Tighter financial conditions usually pressure CHINA50; easier conditions usually support risk appetite and valuation support.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for CHINA50. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production

factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on CHINA50. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on CHINA50. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on CHINA50. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on CHINA50. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on CHINA50. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

scoreChangeLog.summary: CHINA50 is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: Hang Seng Fundamental Pressure
assets.mainConflict: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +1.00) vs Fed Treasury General Account (treasury, -0.48)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 11 counted rows. Top driver: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +1.00). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Higher policy rates usually pressure HSI by raising discount rates and tightening liquidity; easier policy usually helps.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising real yields usually pressure HSI by increasing discount-rate pressure; falling real yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect HSI through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hotter PCE usually pressures HSI through tighter policy expectations; cooler PCE usually supports risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger growth usually supports HSI through earnings and demand, unless it also pushes yields too high.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger consumer demand supports HSI through earnings and growth; weak demand pressures risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider credit spreads usually pressure HSI by signaling funding stress and risk-off conditions; tighter spreads usually support risk.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Primary effect: Tighter financial conditions usually pressure HSI; easier conditions usually support risk appetite and valuation support.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for HSI. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product supplied / demand
factors.derived: EIA product supplied for gasoline, distillates, and total products
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on HSI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on HSI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on HSI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on HSI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HSI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

scoreChangeLog.summary: HSI is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: Emerging Markets Fundamental Pressure
assets.mainConflict: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +1.00) vs Fed Treasury General Account (treasury, -0.48)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 11 counted rows. Top driver: St. Louis financial stress (fed, +1.00). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Higher policy rates usually pressure EM by raising discount rates and tightening liquidity; easier policy usually helps.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising real yields usually pressure EM by increasing discount-rate pressure; falling real yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect EM through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hotter PCE usually pressures EM through tighter policy expectations; cooler PCE usually supports risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger growth usually supports EM through earnings and demand, unless it also pushes yields too high.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger consumer demand supports EM through earnings and growth; weak demand pressures risk appetite.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider credit spreads usually pressure EM by signaling funding stress and risk-off conditions; tighter spreads usually support risk.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Primary effect: Tighter financial conditions usually pressure EM; easier conditions usually support risk appetite and valuation support.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for EM. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product supplied / demand
factors.derived: EIA product supplied for gasoline, distillates, and total products
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on EM. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on EM. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on EM. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on EM. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EM. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Primary effect: The yield curve affects HY Spread through growth expectations, policy tightness, and recession-risk signaling.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Real yields affect HY Spread through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect HY Spread through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: PCE affects HY Spread through the Fed-policy and inflation-expectations channel.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Growth affects HY Spread through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through broad macro pressure.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider credit spreads usually pressure HY Spread by signaling funding stress and risk-off conditions; tighter spreads usually support risk.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Primary effect: Financial conditions affect HY Spread through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for HY Spread. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product supplied / demand
factors.derived: EIA product supplied for gasoline, distillates, and total products
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on HY Spread. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on HY Spread. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on HY Spread. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on HY Spread. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Real yields affect IG Spread through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect IG Spread through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: PCE affects IG Spread through the Fed-policy and inflation-expectations channel.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Growth affects IG Spread through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on IG Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through broad macro pressure.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider credit spreads usually pressure IG Spread by signaling funding stress and risk-off conditions; tighter spreads usually support risk.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Primary effect: Financial conditions affect IG Spread through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for IG Spread. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on IG Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on IG Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on IG Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product supplied / demand
factors.derived: EIA product supplied for gasoline, distillates, and total products
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on IG Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on IG Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on IG Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on IG Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on IG Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on IG Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on IG Spread. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on IG Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on IG Spread. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on IG Spread. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on IG Spread. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on IG Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on IG Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
