factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Growth affects FCI through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through broad macro pressure.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider credit spreads usually pressure FCI by signaling funding stress and risk-off conditions; tighter spreads usually support risk.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Primary effect: Financial conditions affect FCI through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for FCI. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product supplied / demand
factors.derived: EIA product supplied for gasoline, distillates, and total products
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on FCI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on FCI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on FCI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on FCI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

assets.name: Gold Futures / XAUUSD
assets.mainConflict: COT spec direction (cot, +1.30) vs BLS CPI energy pressure (bls, -0.99)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows negative raw pressure from 25 counted rows. Top driver: COT spec direction (cot, +1.30). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gold. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gold. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gold. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gold. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising real yields usually pressure gold because gold pays no yield; falling real yields usually support gold.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising breakevens can support gold if real yields do not rise faster; falling breakevens weaken the inflation-hedge case.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hotter PCE can help gold as inflation pressure only if real yields do not rise; Fed-driven real-yield pressure can outweigh it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Context only: Growth affects Gold through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gold. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through broad macro pressure.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Wider credit spreads can support gold through safe-haven demand, but only if real-yield pressure does not offset it.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for GOLD - COMMODITY EXCHANGE INC. on 2026-07-14: Managed Money long 136,905, short 16,126, net 120,779 (31.48% of open interest), effective for this asset 31.48%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is supportive for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 31.48% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change 4,618; effective weekly change 1.20% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Gold Futures / XAUUSD. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 79.20%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -19,149 (-4.99% of open interest); recent percentile 86.80%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For Gold Futures / XAUUSD, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning 31.48%; commercial/hedger positioning -4.99%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is present for Gold Futures / XAUUSD. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

assets.name: Silver Futures
assets.mainConflict: COT spec direction (cot, +0.65) vs BLS CPI energy pressure (bls, -0.99)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows negative raw pressure from 25 counted rows. Top driver: BLS CPI energy pressure (bls, -0.99). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Silver. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Silver. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Silver. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Silver. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Real yields affect Silver through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for SILVER - COMMODITY EXCHANGE INC. on 2026-07-14: Managed Money long 17,554, short 6,053, net 11,501 (10.95% of open interest), effective for this asset 10.95%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is supportive for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 10.95% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change -1,700; effective weekly change -1.62% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Silver Futures. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 45.30%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -12,708 (-12.10% of open interest); recent percentile 96.20%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For Silver Futures, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning 10.95%; commercial/hedger positioning -12.10%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is present for Silver Futures. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

assets.name: Copper Futures
assets.mainConflict: COT spec direction (cot, +1.30) vs Census building permits (census, -1.16)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 5 counted rows. Top driver: COT spec direction (cot, +1.30). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Copper. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Copper. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Copper. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Copper. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Real yields affect Copper through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Rising breakevens can support Copper as inflation pressure, but demand and supply fundamentals still dominate. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for COPPER- #1 - COMMODITY EXCHANGE INC. on 2026-07-14: Managed Money long 77,338, short 17,153, net 60,185 (23.16% of open interest), effective for this asset 23.16%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is supportive for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 23.16% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change 1,401; effective weekly change 0.54% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Copper Futures. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 56.60%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -86,384 (-33.24% of open interest); recent percentile 47.20%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For Copper Futures, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning 23.16%; commercial/hedger positioning -33.24%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is present for Copper Futures. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

scoreChangeLog.summary: Copper moved +2.3 points versus the previous saved snapshot; current primary state is Mixed / neutral pressure.

assets.name: WTI Crude Oil
assets.mainConflict: EIA crude inventories (eia, +1.35) vs NOAA cold / freeze stress (noaa, -0.75)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 11 counted rows. Top driver: EIA crude inventories (eia, +1.35). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Context only: Policy rates affect WTI Oil mainly through currency, financing-cost, and demand pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Context only: Rate differentials affect WTI Oil indirectly through currency pressure, capital flows, and discount-rate expectations. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Context only: Real-yield differentials matter through inflation-adjusted return; rising real yields usually pressure gold and risk assets but can support the dollar. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Context only: The yield curve affects WTI Oil through growth expectations, policy tightness, and recession-risk signaling. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Context only: Real yields affect WTI Oil through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Rising breakevens can support WTI Oil as inflation pressure, but demand and supply fundamentals still dominate.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Context only: PCE affects WTI Oil through the Fed-policy and inflation-expectations channel. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger growth usually supports WTI Oil by improving demand expectations; weaker growth pressures demand.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger demand can support WTI Oil if it implies more fuel and transportation use; weak demand pressures the energy read. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Context only: Credit spreads affect WTI Oil through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Financial conditions affect WTI Oil through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for WTI Oil. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Cushing stocks are especially important for WTI-linked reads; falling Cushing inventories usually tighten WTI conditions, while builds pressure WTI.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising gasoline/distillate stocks can pressure fuel and crude demand reads; draws usually support the energy complex.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Primary effect: Higher refinery utilization usually supports crude demand; lower utilization can weaken crude demand even if headline inventories look supportive.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising U.S. production adds supply and can pressure oil/gas; falling production can tighten balance and support prices fundamentally.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Primary effect: Trade flows explain inventory pressure. Higher exports can tighten domestic balances; higher imports can loosen them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Secondary effect: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on WTI Oil. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Low direct effect on WTI Oil. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on WTI Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on WTI Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on WTI Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on WTI Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on WTI Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on WTI Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger biofuel demand supports corn/soy oil demand; weaker biofuel demand reduces support. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for WTI-PHYSICAL - NEW YORK MERCANTILE EXCHANGE on 2026-07-14: Managed Money long 181,161, short 119,187, net 61,974 (3.30% of open interest), effective for this asset 3.30%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is supportive for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 3.30% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change -2,067; effective weekly change -0.11% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for WTI Crude Oil. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 54.70%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net 399,298 (21.29% of open interest); recent percentile 100.00%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For WTI Crude Oil, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning 3.30%; commercial/hedger positioning 21.29%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is not elevated for WTI Crude Oil. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.effect: Direct effect: crude inventory draws usually support WTI because visible U.S. supply tightens; builds usually pressure WTI.

factors.effect: Direct effect: Cushing stocks matter strongly for WTI because Cushing is the delivery/storage hub tied to the benchmark.

scoreChangeLog.summary: WTI Oil moved +2.5 points versus the previous saved snapshot; current primary state is Mixed / neutral pressure.

assets.name: Brent Crude Oil
assets.mainConflict: EIA crude inventories (eia, +0.84) vs NOAA cold / freeze stress (noaa, -0.75)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows negative raw pressure from 6 counted rows. Top driver: EIA crude inventories (eia, +0.84). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Context only: Policy rates affect Brent Oil mainly through currency, financing-cost, and demand pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Context only: Rate differentials affect Brent Oil indirectly through currency pressure, capital flows, and discount-rate expectations. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Context only: Real-yield differentials matter through inflation-adjusted return; rising real yields usually pressure gold and risk assets but can support the dollar. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Context only: The yield curve affects Brent Oil through growth expectations, policy tightness, and recession-risk signaling. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Context only: Real yields affect Brent Oil through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Rising breakevens can support Brent Oil as inflation pressure, but demand and supply fundamentals still dominate.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Context only: PCE affects Brent Oil through the Fed-policy and inflation-expectations channel. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger growth usually supports Brent Oil by improving demand expectations; weaker growth pressures demand.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger demand can support Brent Oil if it implies more fuel and transportation use; weak demand pressures the energy read. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Context only: Credit spreads affect Brent Oil through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Financial conditions affect Brent Oil through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for Brent Oil. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Cushing stocks are especially important for WTI-linked reads; falling Cushing inventories usually tighten WTI conditions, while builds pressure WTI.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising gasoline/distillate stocks can pressure fuel and crude demand reads; draws usually support the energy complex.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Primary effect: Higher refinery utilization usually supports crude demand; lower utilization can weaken crude demand even if headline inventories look supportive.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising U.S. production adds supply and can pressure oil/gas; falling production can tighten balance and support prices fundamentally.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Primary effect: Trade flows explain inventory pressure. Higher exports can tighten domestic balances; higher imports can loosen them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Secondary effect: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on Brent Oil. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Low direct effect on Brent Oil. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Brent Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Brent Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Brent Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Brent Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Brent Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Brent Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger biofuel demand supports corn/soy oil demand; weaker biofuel demand reduces support. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for BRENT LAST DAY - NEW YORK MERCANTILE EXCHANGE on 2026-07-14: Managed Money long 13,141, short 604, net 12,537 (5.07% of open interest), effective for this asset 5.07%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is supportive for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 5.07% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change 181; effective weekly change 0.07% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Brent Crude Oil. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 47.20%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -39,226 (-15.87% of open interest); recent percentile 9.40%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For Brent Crude Oil, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning 5.07%; commercial/hedger positioning -15.87%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is present for Brent Crude Oil. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

scoreChangeLog.summary: Brent Oil is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: Natural Gas Futures
assets.mainConflict: Low conflict among counted live rows
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows negative raw pressure from 6 counted rows. Top driver: EIA natural gas storage (eia, -1.35). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Context only: Policy rates affect Natural Gas mainly through currency, financing-cost, and demand pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Context only: Rate differentials affect Natural Gas indirectly through currency pressure, capital flows, and discount-rate expectations. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Context only: Real-yield differentials matter through inflation-adjusted return; rising real yields usually pressure gold and risk assets but can support the dollar. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Context only: The yield curve affects Natural Gas through growth expectations, policy tightness, and recession-risk signaling. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Context only: Real yields affect Natural Gas through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Rising breakevens can support Natural Gas as inflation pressure, but demand and supply fundamentals still dominate.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Context only: PCE affects Natural Gas through the Fed-policy and inflation-expectations channel. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger growth usually supports Natural Gas by improving demand expectations; weaker growth pressures demand. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger demand can support Natural Gas if it implies more fuel and transportation use; weak demand pressures the energy read. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Context only: Credit spreads affect Natural Gas through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Financial conditions affect Natural Gas through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for Natural Gas. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Low direct effect on Natural Gas. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Low direct effect on Natural Gas. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Low direct effect on Natural Gas. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Low direct effect on Natural Gas. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising U.S. production adds supply and can pressure oil/gas; falling production can tighten balance and support prices fundamentally.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Context only: Trade flows explain inventory pressure. Higher exports can tighten domestic balances; higher imports can loosen them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Low direct effect on Natural Gas. Watch it only if it spills into rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Primary effect: Hot/cold weather raises cooling/heating demand and usually supports natural gas; mild weather usually pressures demand.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Primary effect: Higher LNG exports or power burn supports natural gas demand; weaker flows reduce demand pressure.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Natural Gas. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Natural Gas. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Natural Gas. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Natural Gas. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Natural Gas. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Natural Gas. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger biofuel demand supports corn/soy oil demand; weaker biofuel demand reduces support. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for NAT GAS NYME - NEW YORK MERCANTILE EXCHANGE on 2026-07-14: Managed Money long 227,169, short 332,878, net -105,709 (-6.36% of open interest), effective for this asset -6.36%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net short in the related commodity futures contract, which is pressuring for this commodity. Effective positioning equals -6.36% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change -45,414; effective weekly change -2.73% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Natural Gas Futures. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 18.90%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -28,132 (-1.69% of open interest); recent percentile 26.40%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For Natural Gas Futures, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning -6.36%; commercial/hedger positioning -1.69%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is not elevated for Natural Gas Futures. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: EIA natural gas storage
factors.derived: Lower 48 working natural gas in underground storage (png.nw2_epg0_swo_r48_bcf.w) latest 3,024 bcf on 2026-07-10; previous 2,983 bcf on 2026-07-03. Weekly change: 41.0 bcf. Storage versus five-year average: 6.4%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: lower-than-normal gas storage supports natural gas because the supply cushion is tighter; above-normal storage pressures natural gas.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA crude inventories
factors.derived: U.S. commercial crude oil stocks excluding SPR (W_EPC0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest 499,740 thousand barrels on 2016-09-30; previous 502,716 thousand barrels on 2016-09-23. Weekly change: -2,976 thousand barrels.
factors.effect: Low direct effect: crude inventories do not normally drive natural gas. They matter only through broad energy/inflation context.
