scoreChangeLog.summary: Natural Gas is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: RBOB Gasoline
assets.mainConflict: EIA crude inventories (eia, +0.84) vs NOAA cold / freeze stress (noaa, -0.75)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows negative raw pressure from 8 counted rows. Top driver: EIA crude inventories (eia, +0.84). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Context only: Policy rates affect Gasoline mainly through currency, financing-cost, and demand pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Context only: Rate differentials affect Gasoline indirectly through currency pressure, capital flows, and discount-rate expectations. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Context only: Real-yield differentials matter through inflation-adjusted return; rising real yields usually pressure gold and risk assets but can support the dollar. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Context only: The yield curve affects Gasoline through growth expectations, policy tightness, and recession-risk signaling. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Context only: Real yields affect Gasoline through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Rising breakevens can support Gasoline as inflation pressure, but demand and supply fundamentals still dominate.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Context only: PCE affects Gasoline through the Fed-policy and inflation-expectations channel. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger growth usually supports Gasoline by improving demand expectations; weaker growth pressures demand.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger demand can support Gasoline if it implies more fuel and transportation use; weak demand pressures the energy read. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Context only: Credit spreads affect Gasoline through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Financial conditions affect Gasoline through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for Gasoline. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Cushing stocks are especially important for WTI-linked reads; falling Cushing inventories usually tighten WTI conditions, while builds pressure WTI.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising gasoline/distillate stocks can pressure fuel and crude demand reads; draws usually support the energy complex.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Primary effect: Higher refinery utilization usually supports crude demand; lower utilization can weaken crude demand even if headline inventories look supportive.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising U.S. production adds supply and can pressure oil/gas; falling production can tighten balance and support prices fundamentally.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Primary effect: Trade flows explain inventory pressure. Higher exports can tighten domestic balances; higher imports can loosen them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Secondary effect: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on Gasoline. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Low direct effect on Gasoline. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gasoline. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gasoline. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gasoline. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gasoline. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gasoline. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gasoline. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger biofuel demand supports corn/soy oil demand; weaker biofuel demand reduces support. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for GASOLINE RBOB - NEW YORK MERCANTILE EXCHANGE on 2026-07-14: Managed Money long 82,375, short 13,650, net 68,725 (21.17% of open interest), effective for this asset 21.17%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is supportive for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 21.17% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change -2,524; effective weekly change -0.78% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for RBOB Gasoline. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 83.00%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -93,100 (-28.68% of open interest); recent percentile 5.70%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For RBOB Gasoline, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning 21.17%; commercial/hedger positioning -28.68%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is present for RBOB Gasoline. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: EIA gasoline inventories
factors.derived: U.S. motor gasoline stocks (W_EPM0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None.
factors.effect: Direct effect: gasoline inventory draws usually support gasoline because product supply tightens; builds usually pressure gasoline.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA crude inventories
factors.derived: U.S. commercial crude oil stocks excluding SPR (W_EPC0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest 499,740 thousand barrels on 2016-09-30; previous 502,716 thousand barrels on 2016-09-23. Weekly change: -2,976 thousand barrels.
factors.effect: Secondary effect: crude draws can support input-cost and energy-complex context, but gasoline stocks are the more direct input.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing, Oklahoma crude oil stocks (W_EPC0_SAX_YCUOK_MBBL) latest 20,044 thousand barrels on 2026-07-10; previous 19,614 thousand barrels on 2026-07-03. Weekly change: 430.0 thousand barrels.
factors.effect: Low direct effect: Cushing is crude-specific; it matters for gasoline only through crude-market context.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA distillate inventories
factors.derived: U.S. distillate fuel oil stocks (W_EPD0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: distillate stocks help read overall refined-product tightness but are not the main gasoline driver.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA gasoline product supplied
factors.derived: U.S. finished motor gasoline product supplied (WGFUPUS2) latest 8,844 thousand barrels per day on 2026-07-10; previous 8,845 thousand barrels per day on 2026-07-03. Weekly change: -1.0 thousand barrels per day.
factors.effect: Direct effect: stronger gasoline product supplied is a demand proxy that supports gasoline; weaker product supplied pressures demand context.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA refinery utilization
factors.derived: U.S. refinery utilization rate (WPULEUS3) latest 96.2 percent on 2026-07-10; previous 95.8 percent on 2026-07-03. Weekly change: 0.4 percent.
factors.effect: Secondary effect: higher refinery utilization can add product supply, but also indicates stronger refinery demand for crude.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: BLS CPI pressure
factors.derived: CPI all items (CUSR0000SA0) latest 332.57 on 2026-06-01; year-over-year change 3.73%; 3-month annualized pace 2.78%.

scoreChangeLog.summary: Gasoline is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: Heating Oil / Distillates
assets.mainConflict: EIA crude inventories (eia, +0.84) vs EIA distillate product supplied (eia, -2.70)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows negative raw pressure from 8 counted rows. Top driver: EIA distillate product supplied (eia, -2.70). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Context only: Policy rates affect Heating Oil mainly through currency, financing-cost, and demand pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Context only: Rate differentials affect Heating Oil indirectly through currency pressure, capital flows, and discount-rate expectations. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Context only: Real-yield differentials matter through inflation-adjusted return; rising real yields usually pressure gold and risk assets but can support the dollar. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Context only: The yield curve affects Heating Oil through growth expectations, policy tightness, and recession-risk signaling. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Context only: Real yields affect Heating Oil through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Rising breakevens can support Heating Oil as inflation pressure, but demand and supply fundamentals still dominate.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Context only: PCE affects Heating Oil through the Fed-policy and inflation-expectations channel. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger growth usually supports Heating Oil by improving demand expectations; weaker growth pressures demand.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger demand can support Heating Oil if it implies more fuel and transportation use; weak demand pressures the energy read. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Context only: Credit spreads affect Heating Oil through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Financial conditions affect Heating Oil through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for Heating Oil. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Cushing stocks are especially important for WTI-linked reads; falling Cushing inventories usually tighten WTI conditions, while builds pressure WTI.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising gasoline/distillate stocks can pressure fuel and crude demand reads; draws usually support the energy complex.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Primary effect: Higher refinery utilization usually supports crude demand; lower utilization can weaken crude demand even if headline inventories look supportive.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising U.S. production adds supply and can pressure oil/gas; falling production can tighten balance and support prices fundamentally.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Primary effect: Trade flows explain inventory pressure. Higher exports can tighten domestic balances; higher imports can loosen them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Secondary effect: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Context only: Hot/cold weather raises cooling/heating demand and usually supports natural gas; mild weather usually pressures demand. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Context only: Higher LNG exports or power burn supports natural gas demand; weaker flows reduce demand pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Heating Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Heating Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Heating Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Heating Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Heating Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Heating Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger biofuel demand supports corn/soy oil demand; weaker biofuel demand reduces support. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for NY HARBOR ULSD - NEW YORK MERCANTILE EXCHANGE on 2026-07-14: Managed Money long 36,372, short 25,667, net 10,705 (4.07% of open interest), effective for this asset 4.07%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is supportive for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 4.07% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change 5,919; effective weekly change 2.25% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Heating Oil / Distillates. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 34.00%.

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -74,824 (-28.47% of open interest); recent percentile 35.80%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For Heating Oil / Distillates, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict

factors.effect: COT conflict is present for Heating Oil / Distillates. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Context only: Policy rates affect CMD-FUND mainly through currency, financing-cost, and demand pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Context only: Rate differentials affect CMD-FUND indirectly through currency pressure, capital flows, and discount-rate expectations. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Context only: Real-yield differentials matter through inflation-adjusted return; rising real yields usually pressure gold and risk assets but can support the dollar. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Financial conditions affect CMD-FUND through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for CMD-FUND. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Context only: Cushing stocks are especially important for WTI-linked reads; falling Cushing inventories usually tighten WTI conditions, while builds pressure WTI. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Context only: Rising gasoline/distillate stocks can pressure fuel and crude demand reads; draws usually support the energy complex. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Context only: Higher refinery utilization usually supports crude demand; lower utilization can weaken crude demand even if headline inventories look supportive. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Context only: Rising U.S. production adds supply and can pressure oil/gas; falling production can tighten balance and support prices fundamentally. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Context only: Trade flows explain inventory pressure. Higher exports can tighten domestic balances; higher imports can loosen them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Secondary effect: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Context only: Hot/cold weather raises cooling/heating demand and usually supports natural gas; mild weather usually pressures demand. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Context only: Higher LNG exports or power burn supports natural gas demand; weaker flows reduce demand pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Primary effect: Lower ending stocks usually support the crop market because supply cushion is thinner; higher stocks usually pressure it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Context only: A lower stock/use ratio means tighter supply relative to demand and usually supports the crop; a higher ratio usually pressures it. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Context only: Poor crop condition or delayed progress usually supports crop prices; strong conditions and smooth progress usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger export sales support demand for the crop; weak sales pressure the demand read. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Adverse weather or drought usually supports crop prices by threatening supply; favorable weather usually pressures the supply-risk premium.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Context only: Larger competing supply can pressure the crop; supply problems in major exporters usually support it. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger biofuel demand supports corn/soy oil demand; weaker biofuel demand reduces support. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

assets.name: Wheat Futures
assets.mainConflict: USDA production estimate (usda, +2.70) vs NOAA cold / freeze stress (noaa, -1.05)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 3 counted rows. Top driver: USDA production estimate (usda, +2.70). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Context only: Real yields affect Wheat through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through inflation expectations, policy-rate expectations, or real-yield pressure.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through inflation expectations, policy-rate expectations, or real-yield pressure.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Growth affects Wheat through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through broad macro pressure.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through risk appetite, credit stress, or liquidity conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through risk appetite, credit stress, or liquidity conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for Wheat. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Wheat. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Primary effect: Lower ending stocks usually support the crop market because supply cushion is thinner; higher stocks usually pressure it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Primary effect: A lower stock/use ratio means tighter supply relative to demand and usually supports the crop; a higher ratio usually pressures it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Primary effect: Poor crop condition or delayed progress usually supports crop prices; strong conditions and smooth progress usually pressure them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger export sales support demand for the crop; weak sales pressure the demand read.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Primary effect: Adverse weather or drought usually supports crop prices by threatening supply; favorable weather usually pressures the supply-risk premium.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Primary effect: Larger competing supply can pressure the crop; supply problems in major exporters usually support it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger biofuel demand supports corn/soy oil demand; weaker biofuel demand reduces support.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

scoreChangeLog.summary: Wheat is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: Corn Futures
assets.mainConflict: COT spec weekly change (cot, +0.65) vs USDA production estimate (usda, -2.70)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows negative raw pressure from 5 counted rows. Top driver: USDA production estimate (usda, -2.70). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Context only: Real yields affect Corn through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through inflation expectations, policy-rate expectations, or real-yield pressure.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through inflation expectations, policy-rate expectations, or real-yield pressure.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Growth affects Corn through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through broad macro pressure.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through risk appetite, credit stress, or liquidity conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through risk appetite, credit stress, or liquidity conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for Corn. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Corn. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Primary effect: Lower ending stocks usually support the crop market because supply cushion is thinner; higher stocks usually pressure it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Primary effect: A lower stock/use ratio means tighter supply relative to demand and usually supports the crop; a higher ratio usually pressures it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Primary effect: Poor crop condition or delayed progress usually supports crop prices; strong conditions and smooth progress usually pressure them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger export sales support demand for the crop; weak sales pressure the demand read.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Primary effect: Adverse weather or drought usually supports crop prices by threatening supply; favorable weather usually pressures the supply-risk premium.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Primary effect: Larger competing supply can pressure the crop; supply problems in major exporters usually support it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger biofuel demand supports corn/soy oil demand; weaker biofuel demand reduces support.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for CORN - CHICAGO BOARD OF TRADE on 2026-07-14: Managed Money long 294,328, short 282,967, net 11,361 (0.67% of open interest), effective for this asset 0.67%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is neutral for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 0.67% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change 26,360; effective weekly change 1.54% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Corn Futures. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 67.90%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -417,100 (-24.43% of open interest); recent percentile 24.50%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For Corn Futures, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning 0.67%; commercial/hedger positioning -24.43%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is not elevated for Corn Futures. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: EIA petroleum inventory balance
factors.derived: U.S. motor gasoline stocks (W_EPM0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None. | U.S. commercial crude oil stocks excluding SPR (W_EPC0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest 499,740 thousand barrels on 2016-09-30; previous 502,716 thousand barrels on 2016-09-23. Weekly change: -2,976 thousand barrels.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: fuel-demand and energy-market tightness can affect Corn Futures through biofuel economics, transport costs, and inflation pressure, but crop supply/demand reports are more direct.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA natural gas storage
factors.derived: Lower 48 working natural gas in underground storage (png.nw2_epg0_swo_r48_bcf.w) latest 3,024 bcf on 2026-07-10; previous 2,983 bcf on 2026-07-03. Weekly change: 41.0 bcf. Storage versus five-year average: 6.4%.
factors.effect: Low direct effect: natural gas storage does not normally drive Corn Futures except through fertilizer, energy-cost, or broad inflation channels.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: USDA crop condition
factors.derived: USDA/NASS Corn crop condition for None: good/excellent missing (good missing, excellent missing); poor/very poor 0%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: For Corn Futures, weak crop condition usually supports prices because it raises supply-risk concerns; strong crop condition usually pressures prices because the supply outlook improves.
factors.source: USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service Quick Stats API

factors.name: USDA production estimate
factors.derived: USDA/NASS Corn production: 70,101,290,000.0 $ in 2025 vs 63,395,060,000.0 $ in 2024; change 10.6%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: For Corn Futures, larger production usually pressures prices because available supply increases; smaller production usually supports prices because the supply cushion tightens.
factors.source: USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service Quick Stats API

factors.name: USDA crop progress context
factors.derived: USDA/NASS crop progress observation missing.
factors.effect: Direct/context effect: For Corn Futures, delayed planting, emergence, or harvest can support prices by increasing supply risk; smooth progress can reduce risk premium. This row is neutral until compared with normal pace.
factors.source: USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service Quick Stats API
