assets.name: Soybean Futures
assets.mainConflict: COT spec direction (cot, +0.65) vs NOAA cold / freeze stress (noaa, -1.05)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows negative raw pressure from 5 counted rows. Top driver: NOAA cold / freeze stress (noaa, -1.05). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Context only: Real yields affect Soybeans through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through inflation expectations, policy-rate expectations, or real-yield pressure.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through inflation expectations, policy-rate expectations, or real-yield pressure.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Growth affects Soybeans through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through broad macro pressure.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through risk appetite, credit stress, or liquidity conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through risk appetite, credit stress, or liquidity conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for Soybeans. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Soybeans. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Primary effect: Lower ending stocks usually support the crop market because supply cushion is thinner; higher stocks usually pressure it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Primary effect: A lower stock/use ratio means tighter supply relative to demand and usually supports the crop; a higher ratio usually pressures it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Primary effect: Poor crop condition or delayed progress usually supports crop prices; strong conditions and smooth progress usually pressure them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger export sales support demand for the crop; weak sales pressure the demand read.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Primary effect: Adverse weather or drought usually supports crop prices by threatening supply; favorable weather usually pressures the supply-risk premium.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Primary effect: Larger competing supply can pressure the crop; supply problems in major exporters usually support it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger biofuel demand supports corn/soy oil demand; weaker biofuel demand reduces support.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for SOYBEANS - CHICAGO BOARD OF TRADE on 2026-07-14: Managed Money long 145,930, short 70,739, net 75,191 (7.48% of open interest), effective for this asset 7.48%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is supportive for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 7.48% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change 5,612; effective weekly change 0.56% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Soybean Futures. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 45.30%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -207,561 (-20.66% of open interest); recent percentile 60.40%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For Soybean Futures, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning 7.48%; commercial/hedger positioning -20.66%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is present for Soybean Futures. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: EIA petroleum inventory balance
factors.derived: U.S. motor gasoline stocks (W_EPM0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None. | U.S. commercial crude oil stocks excluding SPR (W_EPC0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest 499,740 thousand barrels on 2016-09-30; previous 502,716 thousand barrels on 2016-09-23. Weekly change: -2,976 thousand barrels.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: fuel-demand and energy-market tightness can affect Soybean Futures through biofuel economics, transport costs, and inflation pressure, but crop supply/demand reports are more direct.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA natural gas storage
factors.derived: Lower 48 working natural gas in underground storage (png.nw2_epg0_swo_r48_bcf.w) latest 3,024 bcf on 2026-07-10; previous 2,983 bcf on 2026-07-03. Weekly change: 41.0 bcf. Storage versus five-year average: 6.4%.
factors.effect: Low direct effect: natural gas storage does not normally drive Soybean Futures except through fertilizer, energy-cost, or broad inflation channels.

factors.name: USDA crop condition
factors.derived: USDA/NASS Soybeans crop condition for None: good/excellent missing (good missing, excellent missing); poor/very poor 0%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: For Soybean Futures, weak crop condition usually supports prices because it raises supply-risk concerns; strong crop condition usually pressures prices because the supply outlook improves.
factors.source: USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service Quick Stats API

factors.name: USDA production estimate
factors.derived: USDA/NASS Soybeans production: 43,618,961,000.0 $ in 2025 vs 43,881,999,000.0 $ in 2024; change -0.6%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: For Soybean Futures, larger production usually pressures prices because available supply increases; smaller production usually supports prices because the supply cushion tightens.
factors.source: USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service Quick Stats API

factors.name: USDA crop progress context
factors.derived: USDA/NASS crop progress observation missing.
factors.effect: Direct/context effect: For Soybean Futures, delayed planting, emergence, or harvest can support prices by increasing supply risk; smooth progress can reduce risk premium. This row is neutral until compared with normal pace.
factors.source: USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service Quick Stats API

assets.name: Coffee Futures
assets.mainConflict: COT spec direction (cot, +0.65) vs NOAA cold / freeze stress (noaa, -0.90)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows negative raw pressure from 4 counted rows. Top driver: NOAA cold / freeze stress (noaa, -0.90). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Coffee. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Coffee. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials

factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Coffee. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for COFFEE C - ICE FUTURES U.S. on 2026-07-14: Managed Money long 43,208, short 10,374, net 32,834 (19.68% of open interest), effective for this asset 19.68%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is supportive for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 19.68% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change -1,620; effective weekly change -0.97% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Coffee Futures. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 60.40%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -30,724 (-18.41% of open interest); recent percentile 35.80%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For Coffee Futures, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning 19.68%; commercial/hedger positioning -18.41%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is present for Coffee Futures. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

scoreChangeLog.summary: Coffee is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: Sugar Futures
assets.mainConflict: Low conflict among counted live rows
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows negative raw pressure from 4 counted rows. Top driver: NOAA cold / freeze stress (noaa, -0.90). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through inflation expectations, policy-rate expectations, or real-yield pressure.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through inflation expectations, policy-rate expectations, or real-yield pressure.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Growth affects Sugar through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Consumer demand affects Sugar through growth expectations and policy reaction.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through risk appetite, credit stress, or liquidity conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through risk appetite, credit stress, or liquidity conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for Sugar. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Secondary effect: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Sugar. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger export sales support demand for the crop; weak sales pressure the demand read.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Primary effect: Adverse weather or drought usually supports crop prices by threatening supply; favorable weather usually pressures the supply-risk premium.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Primary effect: Larger competing supply can pressure the crop; supply problems in major exporters usually support it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger biofuel demand supports corn/soy oil demand; weaker biofuel demand reduces support.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for SUGAR NO. 11 - ICE FUTURES U.S. on 2026-07-14: Managed Money long 179,424, short 282,241, net -102,817 (-10.28% of open interest), effective for this asset -10.28%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net short in the related commodity futures contract, which is pressuring for this commodity. Effective positioning equals -10.28% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change 5,833; effective weekly change 0.58% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Sugar Futures. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 83.00%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -131,178 (-13.11% of open interest); recent percentile 5.70%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For Sugar Futures, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning -10.28%; commercial/hedger positioning -13.11%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is not elevated for Sugar Futures. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: EIA petroleum inventory balance
factors.derived: U.S. motor gasoline stocks (W_EPM0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None. | U.S. commercial crude oil stocks excluding SPR (W_EPC0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest 499,740 thousand barrels on 2016-09-30; previous 502,716 thousand barrels on 2016-09-23. Weekly change: -2,976 thousand barrels.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: fuel-demand and energy-market tightness can affect Sugar Futures through biofuel economics, transport costs, and inflation pressure, but crop supply/demand reports are more direct.

factors.effect: Low direct effect: natural gas storage does not normally drive Sugar Futures except through fertilizer, energy-cost, or broad inflation channels.

scoreChangeLog.summary: Sugar is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

assets.name: Cotton Futures
assets.mainConflict: USDA production estimate (usda, +0.90) vs NOAA cold / freeze stress (noaa, -0.90)
assets.quick: Live-only scoring shows positive raw pressure from 5 counted rows. Top driver: USDA production estimate (usda, +0.90). Raw score is uncapped, source-weighted, and excludes non-live rows.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.18% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.55% on 2026-07-17
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury curve spreads on 2026-07-17: 10Y-2Y 0.370 percentage points; 10Y-3M 0.700 percentage points.
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through inflation expectations, policy-rate expectations, or real-yield pressure.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through inflation expectations, policy-rate expectations, or real-yield pressure.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Growth affects Cotton through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Consumer demand affects Cotton through growth expectations and policy reaction.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through risk appetite, credit stress, or liquidity conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through risk appetite, credit stress, or liquidity conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for Cotton. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Primary effect: Lower ending stocks usually support the crop market because supply cushion is thinner; higher stocks usually pressure it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Primary effect: A lower stock/use ratio means tighter supply relative to demand and usually supports the crop; a higher ratio usually pressures it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Primary effect: Poor crop condition or delayed progress usually supports crop prices; strong conditions and smooth progress usually pressure them.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger export sales support demand for the crop; weak sales pressure the demand read.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Primary effect: Adverse weather or drought usually supports crop prices by threatening supply; favorable weather usually pressures the supply-risk premium.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Primary effect: Larger competing supply can pressure the crop; supply problems in major exporters usually support it.
factors.scoreReason: not live freshness: prototype sample / public-source candidate

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Cotton. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for COTTON NO. 2 - ICE FUTURES U.S. on 2026-07-14: Managed Money long 66,685, short 22,759, net 43,926 (13.63% of open interest), effective for this asset 13.63%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is supportive for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 13.63% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change 9,572; effective weekly change 2.97% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Cotton Futures. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 90.60%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is supportive but stretched near the high end of its recent range, so crowded-long unwind risk rises.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -133,903 (-41.55% of open interest); recent percentile 5.70%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For Cotton Futures, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning 13.63%; commercial/hedger positioning -41.55%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is present for Cotton Futures. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
factors.scoreReason: live context row, below scoring threshold

factors.name: EIA petroleum inventory balance
factors.derived: U.S. commercial crude oil stocks excluding SPR (W_EPC0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest 499,740 thousand barrels on 2016-09-30; previous 502,716 thousand barrels on 2016-09-23. Weekly change: -2,976 thousand barrels. | U.S. motor gasoline stocks (W_EPM0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None. | U.S. distillate fuel oil stocks (W_EPD0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None.
factors.effect: Low direct effect: EIA energy data does not normally drive Cotton Futures. It matters mainly through transport, input-cost, or broad inflation channels.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA natural gas storage
factors.derived: Lower 48 working natural gas in underground storage (png.nw2_epg0_swo_r48_bcf.w) latest 3,024 bcf on 2026-07-10; previous 2,983 bcf on 2026-07-03. Weekly change: 41.0 bcf. Storage versus five-year average: 6.4%.
factors.effect: Low direct effect: natural gas storage is not a normal driver of Cotton Futures; crop/weather/export data matters more.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: USDA crop condition
factors.derived: USDA/NASS Cotton crop condition for None: good/excellent missing (good missing, excellent missing); poor/very poor 0%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: For Cotton Futures, weak crop condition usually supports prices because it raises supply-risk concerns; strong crop condition usually pressures prices because the supply outlook improves.
factors.source: USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service Quick Stats API

factors.name: USDA production estimate
factors.derived: USDA/NASS Cotton production: 4,167,893,000.0 $ in 2025 vs 4,561,347,000.0 $ in 2024; change -8.6%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: For Cotton Futures, larger production usually pressures prices because available supply increases; smaller production usually supports prices because the supply cushion tightens.
factors.source: USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service Quick Stats API

factors.name: USDA crop progress context
factors.derived: USDA/NASS crop progress observation missing.
factors.effect: Direct/context effect: For Cotton Futures, delayed planting, emergence, or harvest can support prices by increasing supply risk; smooth progress can reduce risk premium. This row is neutral until compared with normal pace.
factors.source: USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service Quick Stats API

factors.name: NOAA active weather hazards
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS active weather alerts (NWS_ACTIVE_ALERTS) latest 486 active alerts for 2026-07-18; release/effective date 2026-07-17T21:03:00-04:00. Raw lane score treats low active hazard load as positive and elevated weather disruption as negative. Sample events: Air Quality Alert, Small Craft Advisory, Special Weather Statement, Gale Warning, Flood Advisory.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: Severe weather can affect Cotton Futures through logistics and crop-region disruption.
factors.source: NOAA/National Weather Service public weather alerts

factors.effect: Primary effect: Weather stress can affect Cotton Futures through crop condition, yield risk, planting/harvest disruption, and regional supply uncertainty.

scoreChangeLog.summary: Cotton is broadly stable versus the previous saved snapshot.

TREASURY_OFFICIAL.note: Official U.S. Treasury yield curve data. Rates are percentages and this lane is used as public-source rate pressure, not trade timing data.

BLS_PUBLIC.note: BLS inflation/labor macro data applied to 45 assets. Direct for rates, USD, and inflation pressure; secondary/contextual for risk assets and commodities.

BEA_PUBLIC.note: BEA GDP/PCE/growth lane applied to 45 assets. Direct for rates, USD, equities/growth; contextual for commodities. Missing optional observations are not faked.

CFTC_COT.note: CFTC COT refreshed for 27 mapped assets. Interpretation splits directional participation from crowding/contrarian risk.

EIA_OPEN_DATA.note: EIA energy fundamentals applied to 24 assets. Direct for energy commodities; contextual/low relevance for other affected assets.

USDA_PUBLIC.note: USDA/NASS agriculture fundamentals applied to 5 assets. Direct for wheat, corn, soybeans, and cotton; contextual for broad commodity pressure.

NOAA_NWS.note: NOAA/NWS weather-hazard lane applied to 45 assets. Uses public NOAA/NWS active alerts endpoint; no API key required in v0.31.

FED_FRED_SELECTED.note: Federal Reserve/FRED policy and liquidity lane applied to 45 assets. Uses public FRED CSV series for EFFR, WALCL, reserves, RRP, and TGA; no API key required in v0.28.

CENSUS_PUBLIC.label: Census real economy
CENSUS_PUBLIC.note: Retail sales, housing, durable orders, trade, and inventory variables from Census/public economic-indicator series.

FINANCIAL_STRESS_FRED.note: Credit and financial-stress lane applied to 45 assets. Uses public FRED series for HY OAS, IG OAS, BBB OAS, NFCI, ANFCI, and STLFSI4; no API key required in v0.30.

score_history.scoreScale: uncapped raw net pressure; no +/-10 display cap

scope_note: U.S.-centered public-source macro pressure terminal. Raw, uncapped scores rank fundamental pressure across supported assets without using price. Unsupported non-USD crosses remain excluded until direct non-U.S. source lanes exist.
